headline: From Conflict's Wounds to Harvest: Sierra Leone's Farming Revolution
meta_description: A Sierra Leone farming programme teaches amputees sustainable agriculture, transforming lives and building independence after civil war.
summary_short: A pastor-turned-farmer is teaching amputees in Sierra Leone sustainable farming skills, helping them grow food and launch commercial plots.
body:
When Mustapha Bockarie lost his arm to a stray bullet after Sierra Leone's civil war, he felt abandoned. "My friends said I was a burden to them," he recalls. Today, he runs a community farm where he raises goats, grows food to sell, and teaches sustainable practices to his neighbours.

Bockarie's transformation began with Farming on Crutches, a scheme founded in 2018 by Mambud Samai, a pastor who fled the country during the 11-year conflict. The civil war left nearly 30,000 amputees across Sierra Leone with little state support and few work opportunities. After two decades of building community through a sports association for single-leg amputees, Samai pursued formal training in organic farming at Japan's Asian Rural Institute, then returned home determined to create something more.

The programme operates from a small demonstration farm where participants from across the country live and learn together, then carry skills back to their villages. A 2024 cohort even designed an accessible bamboo wheelbarrow using materials already on-site—a practical innovation born from lived experience.

The results speak for themselves. Farming on Crutches recently welcomed its 100th participant, with a growing waitlist and plans to expand across West Africa. "They complete our course and return to their communities as change makers," Samai says. For participants like Bockarie, the message is clear: disability is not inability.
